channel.title: UAP on Alien Truth Social
channel.description: Recent content in UAP on Alien Truth Social

item.title: Pentagon Releases Batch of Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena Files
item.description:
The Pentagon has released approximately 160 declassified documents concerning unidentified anomalous phenomena (UAP), including never-before-seen footage and historical materials dating back to the 1940s. These files contain reports of various UAP sightings, such as “orbs launching orbs” and a “misshapen and uneven ball of white light,” as well as accounts from Apollo crews. The release aims to provide public transparency, though the government has not claimed to have detected extraterrestrial life. Public opinion suggests a belief that the government may be concealing information about UAP, with some thinking they are alien in origin.

Source: https://www.wired.com/story/pentagon-drops-new-ufo-files

item.title: U.S. Department of War Forms 'Space Tiger Team' to Address Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena in Space
item.description:
A Department of War document, obtained via FOIA, reveals the 2023 formation of a “UAP Space Tiger Team” led by the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO). This initiative aims to develop a framework for addressing unidentified anomalous phenomena in space, including spaceborne and transmedium objects. The effort is tied to legislative requirements and involves multiple commands and agencies, such as U.S. Space Command and NORAD. The team’s work is intended to normalize UAP considerations within defense planning and close domain-awareness gaps.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/newly-released-documents-show-uap-space-tiger-team-built-around-space-and-transmedium-cases

item.title: Unidentified flying object
item.description:
An unidentified flying object (UFO), also referred to as an unidentified anomalous phenomenon (UAP), is any aerial object or phenomenon that cannot be immediately identified or explained. While historical reports of unusual aerial sightings date back to ancient times, the term gained cultural prominence following World War II, leading to various government investigations, such as the U.S. Air Force’s Project Blue Book. Although scientific and skeptical organizations, including the Committee for Skeptical Inquiry, consistently find that the majority of these reports are misidentifications of natural phenomena, human technology, or hoaxes, a small percentage remain unexplained. In recent years, entities such as NASA’s UAP independent study team and the Department of Defense’s All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office have continued to analyze data regarding these encounters.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unidentified_flying_object

item.title: Aligned, Multiple-transient Events in the First Palomar Sky Survey
item.description:
In October 2025, astronomer Beatriz Villarroel and psychologist Stephen Bruehl published two companion papers in Publications of the Astronomical Society of the Pacific and Scientific Reports suggesting that transient light events in 1950s Palomar Observatory photographic plates could be linked to nuclear testing or unidentified anomalous phenomena. The research faced widespread skepticism from the scientific community, with experts such as Michael Wiescher and Nigel Hambly suggesting that the anomalies were likely due to plate defects, meteors, or other prosaic environmental factors. A formal rejoinder by physicists Kevin Knuth, Wesley Andres Watters, and Laura Dominé further challenged the authors’ methodology, citing issues with data preparation and a lack of microscopic analysis. While some independent analysis by researchers like Ivo Busko has noted similar transients in other archival datasets, the original papers were notably rejected by the preprint server arXiv for lacking sufficient scholarly rigor.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aligned%2C_Multiple-transient_Events_in_the_First_Palomar_Sky_Survey

item.title: Newly Released Pentagon Records Center on UAP and Internal Email Communications
item.description:
A collection of Department of Defense records has been released, focusing on internal email communications involving Pentagon spokesperson Christopher Sherwood and references to Unidentified Aerial Phenomena (UAP). These documents stem from a Freedom of Information Act request seeking insight into how UAP-related discussions occurred within the Pentagon’s public affairs office. The request specifically targeted emails sent to or from Sherwood containing keywords like “UAP.” While 416 pages were found responsive, portions were withheld under FOIA exemptions related to intelligence information and internal deliberations. The Black Vault has filed an appeal regarding the extensive redactions.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/pentagon-spokesperson-christopher-sherwood-emails-on-ufos-uap-and-more

item.title: Archive of Luis Elizondo’s ‘Deleted’ Emails
item.description:
Luis Elizondo is a central figure in discussions about Unidentified Aerial Phenomena (UAP) due to his alleged involvement with the Pentagon’s Advanced Aerospace Threat Identification Program (AATIP). The Black Vault reported that the Department of Defense (DoD) destroyed Elizondo’s emails, hindering verification of his claims. To circumvent this, The Black Vault pursued FOIA requests for emails of individuals who likely corresponded with Elizondo, indirectly recovering some exchanges. This archive contains these recovered emails, as well as records where “no records” were found, offering insight into Elizondo’s interactions within the DoD.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/archive-of-luis-elizondos-deleted-emails

item.title: FAA Records Detail Pilot Encounter with Unidentified Object Pacing Aircraft Over Nevada
item.description:
Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) records obtained through a FOIA request detail an unusual aerial encounter over Northern Nevada in May 2025 involving a business jet. Air traffic control audio and internal FAA logs document an object that was visually observed, appeared aircraft-like, and maintained a parallel course with the jet, but was not detected on radar. The object was described with evolving terminology, shifting from an undefined “black” object to something resembling a “fighter” before rapidly ascending and disappearing. The FAA withheld radar data, citing Sensitive Security Information, and the encounter occurred in an area near multiple military testing facilities.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/faa-records-detail-pilot-encounter-with-unidentified-object-pacing-aircraft-over-nevada

item.title: FOIA Request on UAP Keywords Produces Minimal Results, Heavy Redactions, from Energy Department
item.description:
A Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request to the U.S. Department of Energy for communications regarding unidentified aerial phenomena (UAP) yielded only two partially released documents. These documents, internal DOE emails from May 2024, were prepared for Secretary Jennifer Granholm in advance of an oversight hearing. The emails reference attached briefing materials that included “UFO and UPA talking points” for congressional engagement. Despite the request’s broad scope, significant portions of the released documents were withheld under FOIA exemptions, preventing the disclosure of substantive content or internal analysis on UAP topics.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/foia-request-on-uap-keywords-produces-minimal-results-heavy-redactions-from-energy-department

item.title: Despite Trump’s Call to Release UAP Files, Navy Denies Appeal for 78 Classified UAP Photographs
item.description:
The U.S. Navy has formally denied an appeal seeking the release of 78 photographs designated as “unidentified aerial phenomena” (UAP). This decision upholds a previous denial of a Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request filed by The Black Vault. The Navy cited classification under Executive Order 13526 and the UAP Classification Guide as the reason for withholding the images. Despite public calls for transparency and a former president’s stated desire to release such files, the Navy’s appeal authority deferred to the Original Classification Authority, stating that no portion of the photographs could be released.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/despite-trumps-call-to-release-uap-files-navy-denies-appeal-for-78-classified-uap-photographs

item.title: Pentagon Refuses to Search for ‘Immaculate Constellation’ Emails
item.description:
The Department of War has refused to conduct a Freedom of Information Act search for emails related to the “Immaculate Constellation” program. This refusal is based on the department’s assertion that the program does not exist, therefore no responsive records could exist. This stance is unusual, as FOIA typically requires agencies to search for records regardless of the perceived validity of the subject matter. The phrase “Immaculate Constellation” entered public discourse in late 2024, leading to congressional attention and an official denial from the Department of Defense. Other agencies, like the NSA, have responded to similar requests with Glomar responses, acknowledging the request without confirming or denying the existence of records.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/pentagon-refuses-to-search-for-immaculate-constellation-emails

item.title: Closed-Door 2024 UAP Briefing: FOIA Discloses Select Media Invitees
item.description:
Newly released records obtained through a Freedom of Information Act request reveal journalists who were privately invited to an embargoed Pentagon media roundtable on Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena (UAP). This event, held on March 6, 2024, was connected to the Department of Defense’s first volume of the congressionally mandated Historical Record Report on U.S. government UAP programs. The invitation, sent by Pentagon spokesperson Susan Gough, outlined strict conditions for participation, including agreeing to an embargo until the report’s public release on March 8. Journalists from outlets such as The New York Times, CNN, Politico, Task & Purpose, and The Washington Post were among those invited, though some recipients remain redacted due to privacy concerns.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/closed-door-2024-uap-briefing-foia-discloses-select-media-invitees
